title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
3 Interactive Data Visualisation - ggiraph methods
ggiraph is a htmlwidget and a ggplot2 extension. It allows ggplot graphics to be dynamic and interactive.
Interactive is made with ggplot geometries that can understand three main aesthetics:

Tooltip: highlights a column of data-sets that contain tooltips to be displayed when the mouse is hovered over specific elements.
Data_id: highlights all data points that share the same ID. Hence when we over over multiple points that have the same ID, they all light up together simultaneously.
Onclick: highlights a column of data-sets that contain a JavaScript function to be executed when elements are clicked.







Note



How does it work?
It extends ggplot2 with:

interactive geom functions: geom_point_interactive(), geom_col_interactive, etc.
interactive scale functions: scale_color_continuous_interactive(), scale_fill_manual_interactive(), etc.
interactive facet functions: facet_wrap_interactive() and facet_grid_interactive() that both work with labeller_interactive().
interactive guide functions: guide_colorbar_interactive(), guide_colourbar_interactive(), guide_legend_interactive().
interactive theme elements: element_line_interactive(), element_rect_interactive(), element_text_interactive(), label_interactive.




3.1 Tooltip Effect

Basic Tooltip (ID)Multiple Info TooltipCustomised Tooltip StyleStatistics on TooltipCoordinated Linked Plots


Below shows a typical code chunk to plot an interactive statistical graph by using ggiraph package. Notice that the code chunk consists of two parts. First, a ggplot object will be created. Next, girafe() of ggiraph will be used to create an interactive svg object.
Notice that two steps are involved. First, an interactive version of ggplot2 geom (i.e. geom_dotplot_interactive()) will be used to create the basic graph. Then, girafe() will be used to generate an svg object to be displayed on an html page.

Steps in creating an interactive graphic:

Instead of geom_point (i.e.), use geom_point_interactive - provide at least one of the aesthetics tools (tooltip, data_id or onclick)
Call function girafe with the ggplot object to translate graphic into a web interactive graphic.


Character format (ID) set as tooltipRow Index set as tooltipNumerical column set as tooltip



We can set the ‘ID’ column in the dataset as tooltip by specifying aes(tooltip=ID).
To display student’s ID (character), you just need to hover your mouse pointer on the data point of interest.
Note that the first point on the extreme left corresponds to Student321 in the ‘ID’ column.



Show the code
p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip=ID),  #<<< Interactivity at the tooltip, aes() maps objects
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth = 1,
                           method = "histodot",
                           fill='seagreen') +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, #<<< Scale can also be interactive
                     breaks= NULL) +  #<<< null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  theme_bw()

girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 6*0.618)









We can set the row index of the data as tooltip by just setting aes(tooltip=row.names(exam_data)).
Note that the student on the extreme left is from row index 1 (i.e. first row) in the exam_data dataset, and that corresponds to Student321 in the ‘ID’ column



Show the code
p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip=row.names(exam_data)),  #<<< Interactivity at the tooltip, aes() maps objects
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth = 1,
                           method = "histodot",
                           fill='plum3') +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, #<<< Scale can also be interactive
                     breaks= NULL) +  #<<< null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  theme_bw()

girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 6*0.618)









We can set a numerical column (here, the ‘MATHS’ column) in the dataset as tooltip. This can be done by setting aes(tooltip=factor(MATHS)).
Note that it is advisable to convert the numerical data to factor first before assigning it to tooltip.
Note that the student on the extreme left is from row index 1 (i.e. first row) in the exam_data dataset, and that corresponds to Student321 in the ‘ID’ column. He/she scored only 9 in his ‘MATHS’ score.



Show the code
p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip=factor(MATHS)),  #<<< Interactivity at the tooltip, aes() maps objects
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth = 1,
                           method = "histodot",
                           fill='turquoise') +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, #<<< Scale can also be interactive
                     breaks= NULL) +  #<<< null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  theme_bw()

girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 6*0.618)












Instead of displaying only one dimension of data at a time - whether it is specific column or specific row - why not display a few of them together at the same time?
This can be done by creating a list of objects as a new column (here, the new column is called tooltip that comprises ‘ID’ and ‘CLASS’ columns in the exam_data dataset), and we then assign that new column as an argument to the tooltip parameter instead i.e. aes(tooltip=tooltip).
If you hover over the first point on the extreme left, you can see the information being displayed for that student telling you that this student corresponds to ID Student321, and he/she is from CLASS 3I.


Show the code
## creating a new column called tooltip
## -------------------------------------
exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0("ID = ",
                              exam_data$ID,
                              "\n Class = ",
                              exam_data$CLASS))

## assigning the new column as an argument to the tooltip parameter
## ----------------------------------------------------------------
p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip=tooltip),  
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth = 1,
                           method = "histodot",
                           fill='navyblue') +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, #<<< Scale can also be interactive
                     breaks= NULL) +  #<<< null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  theme_bw()

girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 6*0.618)








Code chunk below uses opts_tooltip() of ggiraph to customize tooltip rendering by adding css declarations.

Black and bold fontItalic with increased font size



Notice that the background colour of the tooltip is gray and the font colour is black and bold.
This is controlled by specifying the tooltip_css, and then assign it as an argument to the css parameter and then formatting it as a list in the options parameter in the giraffe object (See the code chunk #<<< section on where and how they are inserted).



Show the code
tooltip_css <- 'background-color:#dfdfeb; font-style:bold; color:black;'  #<<<

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip=tooltip),
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth = 1,
                           method = "histodot",
                           fill='tomato') +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks= NULL) +  #null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  theme_bw()
  
girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618,
       options = list(          #<<<
         opts_tooltip(          #<<<
           css=tooltip_css
         )
       ))








We can also change the font-famnily, font-size, color and more. Here’s an example to give a ‘sci-fi’ feel.


Show the code
tooltip_css <- 'background-color:#353638; 
font-style:bold;  
font-weight:500;
font-family: "Michroma", sans-serif;
color:white;
font-size:22px;'  #<<<

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip=tooltip),
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth = 1,
                           method = "histodot",
                           fill='violetred') +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks= NULL) +  #null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  theme_bw()
  
girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618,
       options = list(          #<<<
         opts_tooltip(          #<<<
           css=tooltip_css
         )
       ))






Click [here] (https://www.ardata.fr/ggiraph-book/customize.html) for more information on how to customise ggiraph objects.





Code chunk below shows an advanced way to customise tooltip. In this example, a function is used to compute 90% confident interval of the mean. The derived statistics are then displayed in the tooltip.


Show the code
tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = 0.01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales:: number (ymax-y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores (with standard error):" , mean, "+/-", sem) #<<< Paste the results in tooltip
}


gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
                   aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(aes(y=MATHS,
                   tooltip = after_stat(tooltip(y, ymax))), #<<<
               fun.data = mean_se,
               geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
               fill = 'seagreen') +
  stat_summary(aes(y= MATHS),
               fun.data = mean_se,
               geom= 'errorbar',
               width = 0.2,
               size = 0.2)+
  labs(title="Math Scores by Race",
  y ="Math Scores") + 
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory", color = "ivory"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory", color = "ivory"))

girafe(ggobj=gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8 * 0.618)







Explanation of the codes above:

Tooltip self-defined function:


It takes in two arguments y and ymax from results of stat_summary() via after_stat().
accuracy is a fixed parameter with a value of 0.01. It takes a number to round to, ie 0.01 is used to show 2 decimal places of precision.
scales:: number is to convert number to text, with formatting.
sem output: Since ymax = y + se, thus, sem = ymax-y
It outputs “Mean maths scores (with standard error): 57.44 +/- 2.03”


Stat_summary function creates 2 other geom layers. The first stat summary function has two aes mappings to visual: (1) MATHS as Y (2) tooltip output


It first applies mean_se method to output y, ymin, ymax for each MATHS score by RACE
After this, these groups of 3 values are send into tooltip function via after_stat() helper function
GeomInteractive makes the columns interactive so tooltips is displayed when users hover over


The second stat summary uses y, ymin, ymax obtained from method= mean_se to plot the error bar



Documentation of mean_se:
stat_summary(aes(fun.data=mean_se) is default. mean_se(x, mult = 1). When input a list of values, it returns a data frame with three columns:

y: The mean.
ymin: The mean minus the multiples of the standard error.
ymax: The mean plus the multiples of the standard error.

There are a few summary functions from the Hmisc package which are reformatted for use in stat_summary(). They all return aesthetics for y, ymax, and ymin.

mean_cl_normal(): Returns sample mean and 95% confidence intervals assuming normality (i.e., t-distribution based)
mean_sdl(): Returns sample mean and a confidence interval based on the standard deviation times some constant
mean_cl_boot(): Uses a bootstrap method to determine a confidence interval for the sample mean without assuming normality.
median_hilow(): Returns the median and an upper and lower quantiles.




Coordinated multiple views methods has been implemented in the data visualisation below.
Notice that when a data point of one of the dotplot is selected, the corresponding data point ID on the second data visualisation will be highlighted too.


Show the code
exam_data$tooltip2 <- c(paste0("Name = ",
                              exam_data$ID,
                              "\n Class = ",
                              exam_data$CLASS,
                              "\n English Score = ",
                              exam_data$ENGLISH,
                              "\n Math Score = ",
                              exam_data$MATHS                              
                              ))


p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID,
        tooltip = tooltip2),      #<<< Added          
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot", 
    fill='plum') +  
  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +    #<<< p1 same as p2 x-axis
  
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,            # suppress y axis
                     breaks = NULL)+
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "snow", color = "snow"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "snow", color = "snow")) 

## -----------------------------------------------

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID,
        tooltip = tooltip2),      #<<< Added                
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot",
    fill='lightcoral') + 
  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +    #<<< p1 same as p2 x-axis
  
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL) +
  
  labs(x ="Distribution of English Scores") + 
  
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "snow", color = "snow"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "snow", color = "snow"))

## -----------------------------------------

girafe(code = print(p1 + p2),         #<<< coordinated multiple views
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 3,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
         )
       ) 






In order to build a coordinated multiple views as shown in the example above, the following programming strategy will be used:

Appropriate interactive functions of ggiraph will be used to create the multiple views.
patchwork function of patchwork package will be used inside girafe function to create the interactive coordinated multiple views.

Note: hover effects can be encoded directly in girafe unlike tooltips and data_id . Added tooltip aes effects as well.
The data_id aesthetic is critical to link observations between plots and the tooltip aesthetic is optional but nice to have when mouse over a point.





3.2 Data_id Hover Effect

Basic HoverStyled HoverCombining Tooltip and Hover



Code chunk below shows the second interactive feature of ggiraph, namely data_id.
All elements associated with a data_id (i.e ID) will be highlighted upon mouse over.



Show the code
p <- ggplot(data= exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip = ID,  #<<< Tooltip popup
                               data_id = ID),
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth= 1,
                           method = 'histodot',
                           fill = 'darkgreen'
                           ) +
  scale_y_continuous( NULL,
                      breaks = NULL) + #null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Math Scores") + 
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "seashell", color = "seashell"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "seashell", color = "seashell"))

girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 6 *0.618,
       #options = list(                          #<<<
       #opts_hover(css='fill: green2;'),         #<<<
       #opts_hover_inv(css = 'opacity: 0.2;'))  #<<<
         )








In the code chunk below, css codes are used to change the highlighting effect.
Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over.


Show the code
p <- ggplot(data= exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(data_id = CLASS),
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth= 1,
                           method = 'histodot',
                           fill = 'steelblue'
                           ) +
  scale_y_continuous( NULL,
                      breaks = NULL) + #null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Math Scores") + 
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "seashell", color = "seashell"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "seashell", color = "seashell"))

girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 6 *0.618,
       options = list(                          #<<<
       opts_hover(css='fill: green2;'),         #<<<
       opts_hover_inv(css = 'opacity: 0.2;'))  #<<<
         )






Note: * Different from previous example (tooltip_css is pre-defined as input to a parameter in girafe(options=list(opts_tooltip(css=tooltip_css)))). * In this example the ccs customisation request are encoded directly as girafe(options=list(opts_hover(css='tooltip_css'fill:#202020;'))).


There are times that we want to combine tooltip and hover effect on the interactive statistical graph as shown in the code chunk below.
Elements associated with a data_id (i.e CLASS) will be highlighted upon mouse over. At the same time, the tooltip will show the CLASS.


Show the code
p <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
            aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(aes(tooltip = CLASS,  #<<< Tooltip popup
                               data_id = CLASS), #<<<
                           stackgroups = TRUE,
                           binwidth = 1,
                           method = "histodot",
                           fill='red1') +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,
                     breaks= NULL)+  #null to suppress axis labels
  labs(x ="Distribution of Math Scores") + 
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "honeydew", color = "honeydew"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "honeydew", color = "honeydew"))

girafe(ggobj=p,
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 6*0.618,
       options = list(                          #<<<
         opts_hover(css='fill: gold;'),         #<<<
         opts_hover_inv(css = 'opacity: 0.2;')  #<<<
         )
       )











3.3 Onclick Effect
The onclick argument of ggiraph provides hotlink interactivity on the web.
The code chunk below shown an example of onclick.


Show the code
mtcars_data <- mtcars
mtcars_data$car <- rownames(mtcars)

mtcars_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
  "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/",
  gsub(" ", "_", mtcars_data$car))

p <- ggplot(data=mtcars_data,
       aes(x = wt,
           y = mpg)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = car,
        onclick = onclick),
    size = 3,
    color = 'steelblue') +
  labs(title = "Click on a point to learn more about the car",
       x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
       y = "Miles per Gallon") +
  theme(axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "snow", color = "snow"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "snow", color = "snow"))

girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618)






Note that click actions must be a string column in the dataset containing valid javascript instructions.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
4. Interactive Data Visualisation - Plotly methods
Plotly’s R graphing library create interactive web graphics from ggplot2 graphs and/or a custom interface to the (MIT-licensed) JavaScript library plotly.js inspired by the grammar of graphics. Different from other plotly platform, plot.R is free and open source.
There are two ways to create interactive graph by using plotly, they are:

by using plotly(), and
by using ggplotly()


4.1 Using plotly

Basic scatter plot using plotlyScatter plot + visual variable using plotly


The tabset below shows an example an interactive plot created by using plotly()


Show the code
plot_ly(data = exam_data,
        x = ~MATHS,
        y = ~ENGLISH)








In the code chunk below, color argument is mapped to a qualitative visual variable (i.e. RACE). Click on the colour symbol at the legend to only show the RACE selected.


Show the code
plot_ly(data = exam_data,
        x = ~ENGLISH,
        y = ~MATHS,
        color = ~RACE)











4.2 Using ggplotly

Interactive scatter plot using ggplotly()Coordinated linked plots using ggplotlyUsing sliders


The code chunk below plots an interactive scatter plot by using ggplotly().
Notice that the only extra line you need to include in the code chunk is ggplotly().

BasicCustomised Tooltip




Show the code
p <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x= MATHS,
                y= ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point (size =1) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm)+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(y= 'ENGLISH') +                                    
  theme(axis.title.y=element_text(angle = 0,
                                  vjust=0.9)) +   #<<< does not work in ggplotly
  labs(title = 'English against Math scores', 
       x = 'Math',
       y = 'English') +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
        axis.title.x = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold")
        )+
  theme_bw()

ggplotly(p)








Tooltip includes: Class, StudentID, Gender, Race, Math, and English scores.


Show the code
p_c <- ggplot(data = exam_data,
            aes(x= MATHS,
                y= ENGLISH,
                color=CLASS,
                label1=ID,      #<<<
                label2=GENDER,  #<<<
                label3=RACE,    #<<<
                label4=MATHS,   #<<<
                label5=ENGLISH  #<<<
                )) +
  geom_point (size =1) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm)+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  
  ## set tick marks manually
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 20)) +

  labs(title = 'English against Math scores', 
       x = 'Math',
       y = 'English',
       color="Class") +
  
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
        axis.title.x = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
        axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.8),
        axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm"),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory2", color = "ivory2"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory2", color = "ivory2"),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory2", color = "ivory2")
        )  

ggplotly(p_c, tooltip=c("label1", "label2", "label3", "label4", "label5")) # <<<











The creation of a coordinated linked plot by using plotly involves three steps:

highlight_key() of plotly package is used as shared data.
two scatterplots will be created by using ggplot2 functions.
lastly, subplot() of plotly package is used to place them next to each other side-by-side.



Show the code
library(plotly)
library(ggplot2)

## step 1 - create the highlight_key()
## ------------------------------------
d <- highlight_key(exam_data)

## step 2 - create 2 scaterplots
## ------------------------------------
p1 <- ggplot(data = d,
             aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size = 1, color='royalblue') +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Maths", y = "English") + theme_bw()

p2 <- ggplot(data = d,
             aes(x = MATHS, y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size = 1, color='seagreen') +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(x = "Maths", y = "Science") + theme_bw()

## 3. use subplot() to place the charts side-by-side
## -----------------------------------------------------
subplot(
  ggplotly(p1),
  ggplotly(p2),
  nrows = 1,
  shareX = TRUE
) %>%
  highlight(
    on = "plotly_hover",
    off = "plotly_doubleclick",
    persistent = FALSE,
    dynamic = TRUE,
    color = "navyblue",
    opacityDim = 0.5
  ) %>%
  layout(title = "Side-by-Side Subplots")






Things to learn from the code chunk: * highlight_key() simply creates an object of class crosstalk::SharedData.

Visit this link to learn more about crosstalk



Key arguments: - animation_opts() function here to change properties of the plotly animation - frame controls the amount of time between transitions (in milliseconds) - redraw = FALSE option means that the base plot won’t be redrawn at every transition.
Here’s a plot that shows life expectancy and GDP evolving over time.


Show the code
library(gapminder)

p <- ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, color = continent, size = pop)) +
  
  geom_point(alpha = 0.1) +
  
  geom_point(aes(frame = year, ids = country)) +
  
  labs(x='GDP per Capita in Log Scale', y='Life Expectency') +
  
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10") +
  
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
        axis.title.x = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
        axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.8),
        axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm"),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory2", color = "ivory2"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory2", color = "ivory2"),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = "ivory2", color = "ivory2")
        )  
 

ggplotly(p) %>% 
  animation_opts(1000, redraw = FALSE)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
5 Interactive Data Visualisation - crosstalk methods
Crosstalk is an add-on to the htmlwidgets package. It extends htmlwidgets with a set of classes, functions, and conventions for implementing cross-widget interactions (currently, linked brushing and filtering).

Interactive Data TableLinked Brushing



A wrapper of the JavaScript Library DataTables
Data objects in R can be rendered as HTML tables using the JavaScript library ‘DataTables’ (typically via R Markdown or Shiny).



Show the code
DT::datatable(exam_data %>% 
                select(ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE, ENGLISH, MATHS, SCIENCE),
              class='compact',
              options = list(pageLength = 5))








We can also link the data in the table to the points in the plot.
Try clicking a few rows in the table, and the corresponding points in the chart will show up.


Show the code
exam_clean <- exam_data %>% 
  select(ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE, ENGLISH, MATHS, SCIENCE)

d <- highlight_key(exam_clean)

p <- ggplot(d, aes(ENGLISH, MATHS)) + 
  geom_point(size=1, color='deepskyblue4') +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  theme_bw()

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p), "plotly_selected")

crosstalk::bscols(
  gg, 
  DT::datatable(d,
                class='compact',
                options = list(pageLength = 5)),
  widths = c(7, 5)  
)
















highlight_key(): used to create a unique identifier for each row in a data frame, based on its values. The resulting identifier is used to keep track of the rows that have been selected or highlighted on a plot, particularly when using the highlight() function.
plotly_selected: a built-in plotly attribute that represents the currently selected points on the plot.
So, highlight(ggplotly(p), "plotly_selected") is taking the ggplotly(p) object and highlighting the currently selected points on the plot by changing their appearance in some way, such as by changing their color or size.
crosstalk::bscols(): creates a Bootstrap column layout. The first argument is the left column, and the second argument is the right column. The widths argument is used to specify the relative width of the two columns, with the default value being 6 for both.
crosstalk::bscols(): particularly useful when working with interactive data visualizations, as it allows for easy linking of different components of the dashboard, such as brushing and highlighting on the plot and filtering on the data table.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Visualising distributions has long been a central task in statistical analysis. Common graphical methods include histograms, probability density curves (PDFs), boxplots, notch plots, and violin plots, all of which can be effectively created using ggplot2.
In this section, we introduce two more recent and increasingly popular statistical graphics for visualising distributions—ridgeline plots and raincloud plots—and demonstrate how to create them using ggplot2 and its extensions.

Installing and loading librariesImporting DataUnderstanding the DataConverting data types

In this section, Exam_data.csv provided will be used. Using read_csv() of readr package, import Exam_data.csv into R.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
3 Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot
Ridgeline plot (sometimes called Joyplot) is a data visualisation technique for revealing the distribution of a numeric value for several groups. Distribution can be represented using histograms or density plots, all aligned to the same horizontal scale and presented with a slight overlap.






Note



When to use Ridgeline Plots?

Ridgeline plots make sense when the number of group to represent is medium to high, and thus a classic window separation would take to much space. Indeed, the fact that groups overlap each other allows to use space more efficiently. If you have less than 5 groups, dealing with other distribution plots is probably better.
It works well when there is a clear pattern in the result, like if there is an obvious ranking in groups. Otherwise group will tend to overlap each other, leading to a messy plot not providing any insight.




(a) Basic Plot(b) Colour - Transition(c) Colour - Probability Density(d) Colour - Quantile(e) Colour - Tails


There are several ways to plot ridgeline plot with R. In this section, we will learn how to plot ridgeline plot by using ggridges package.
ggridges package provides two main geom to plot gridgeline plots, they are: geom_ridgeline() and geom_density_ridges(). The former takes height values directly to draw the ridgelines, and the latter first estimates data densities and then draws those using ridgelines.
The ridgeline plot below is plotted by using geom_density_ridges().


Show the code
ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
    ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()+
  theme_bw()











Sometimes we would like to have the area under a ridgeline not filled with a single solid color but rather with colors that vary in some form along the x axis. This effect can be achieved by using either geom_ridgeline_gradient() or geom_density_ridges_gradient(). Both geoms work just like geom_ridgeline() and geom_density_ridges(), except that they allow for varying fill colors. However, they do not allow for alpha transparency in the fill. For technical reasons, we can have changing fill colors or transparency but not both.


Show the code
ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS,
           fill = stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    color='#8da3ca',
    linewidth=1.05) +
  scale_color_continuous_sequential('Purple-Blue')+
  scale_fill_continuous_sequential('Purple-Blue', name='displ')+
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()+
  labs(x = "English Score",
       y = "",
       title="Colour transition from light to dark as scores improve.") +   
  theme_bw()  











Beside providing additional geom objects to support the need to plot ridgeline plot, ggridges package also provides a stat function called stat_density_ridges() that replaces stat_density() of ggplot2.
Figure below is plotted by mapping the probabilities calculated by using stat(ecdf) which represent the empirical cumulative density function for the distribution of English score.


Show the code
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS, 
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5-stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(geom = "density_ridges_gradient", 
                      calc_ecdf = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Tail probability",
                       direction = -1) +
  theme_ridges()+
  labs(x = "English",
       y = "",
       title="Uneven distribution across classes.") +   
  theme_bw()    













Show the code
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS, 
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE, 
    quantiles = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  theme_ridges()+
  labs(x = "English",
       y = "",
       title="Uneven distribution across classes.") +   
  theme_bw()    











Instead of using number to define the quantiles, we can also specify quantiles by cut points such as 2.5% and 97.5% tails to colour the ridgeline plot as shown in the figure below.


Show the code
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH, 
           y = CLASS, 
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE, 
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
    ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  theme_ridges()+
  labs(x = "English",
       y = "",
       title="Uneven distribution across classes.") +   
  theme_bw()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
4 Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot
Raincloud Plot is a data visualisation techniques that produces a half-density to a distribution plot. It gets the name because the density plot is in the shape of a “raincloud”. The raincloud (half-density) plot enhances the traditional box-plot by highlighting multiple modalities (an indicator that groups may exist). The boxplot does not show where densities are clustered, but the raincloud plot does!
In this section, we shall learn how to create a raincloud plot to visualise the distribution of English score by race. It can be achieved using functions provided by ggdist and ggplot2 packages.

(a) Half-Eye Graph(b) Adding Boxplot(c) Adding Dot Plot(d) Coordinate Flip


First, we will plot a Half-Eye graph by using stat_halfeye() of ggdist package.
This produces a Half Eye visualization, which is contains a half-density and a slab-interval.
We remove the slab interval by setting .width = 0 and point_colour = NA.


Show the code
ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = RACE, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA,
               fill="turquoise")+
  labs(x = "",
       y = "English\nScore",
       title="Uneven distribution across classes.") +   
  theme_bw()    











Next, we will add the second geometry layer using geom_boxplot() of ggplot2. This produces a narrow boxplot. We reduce the width and adjust the opacity.


Show the code
ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = RACE, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA,
               fill="turquoise") +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA,
               fill="tan") +
  labs(x = "",
       y = "English\nScore",
       title="Uneven distribution across classes.") +   
  theme_bw()    











Next, we will add the third geometry layer using stat_dots() of ggdist package. This produces a half-dotplot, which is similar to a histogram that indicates the number of samples (number of dots) in each bin. We select side = “left” to indicate we want it on the left-hand side.


Show the code
ggplot(exam, 
       aes(x = RACE, 
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA,
               fill="turquoise") +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA,
               fill="tan") +
    stat_dots(side = "left", 
            justification = 1.2, 
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2,
            color="plum") +
  labs(x = "",
       y = "English\nScore",
       title="Uneven distribution across classes.") +   
  theme_bw()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Visualising uncertainty poses a significant challenge in data visualisation. When we observe a data point placed at a particular location, we often perceive it as an exact reflection of the true data value. It’s hard to grasp that the data point might actually exist within a range beyond where it’s plotted. This situation is common in data visualisation, as nearly every dataset contains some degree of uncertainty. The decisions we make about whether and how to depict this uncertainty can greatly impact how accurately our audience understands the data’s significance.
In this chapter, we shall focus on creating statistical graphics for visualising uncertainty, including:

plotting statistics error bars by using ggplot2,
plotting interactive error bars by combining ggplot2, plotly and DT,
creating advanced by using ggdist, and
creating hypothetical outcome plots (HOPs) by using ungeviz package.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
section: 3. Visualising Uncertainty - using manual methods (ggplot2 and plotly)
text:
3. Visualising Uncertainty - using manual methods (ggplot2 and plotly)
A point estimate is a single number, such as a mean. Uncertainty is expressed as standard error, confidence interval, or credible interval.






Note



Standard Deviation vs Standard Error
Standard Deviation (SD):

Measures how spread out the individual data points are within a single sample
Answers: “How much do individual students’ scores vary from the class average?”
Formula: \(\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}}\)
High SD = scores are widely spread; Low SD = scores are clustered close to the mean

Standard Error (SE):

Measures how reliable your sample mean is as an estimate of the true population mean
Answers: “If I took many different samples, how much would the sample means vary from each other?”
Formula: \(SE = \frac{SD}{\sqrt{n}}\)
High SE = sample mean is unreliable; Low SE = sample mean is a good estimate

Key analogy:
Imagine taking 100 different samples of students and computing the mean score for each sample. Those 100 means will themselves form a distribution — the Standard Error is the SD of that distribution of means. This is what is referred to as the “mother of all means” — the theoretical distribution of all possible sample means.
In simple terms:









Standard Deviation (SD)
Standard Error (SE)




What it measures
Spread of individual values
Spread of sample means


Operates at
Individual data level
Sample mean level


CLT perspective
Describes spread of population or one sample
Describes how much sample means vary around the population mean


Formula
\(s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}}\)
\(SE = \frac{SD}{\sqrt{n}}\)


Effect of larger \(n\)
Not affected
Decreases — larger \(n\) = smaller SE = more reliable mean


Used for
Describing variation in data
Describing uncertainty of the mean estimate


Answers
“How spread out are the individual scores?”
“How reliable is my sample mean as an estimate of the population mean?”






data preprocessing
The code chunk below performs the following:

group_by() of dplyr package groups the observation by RACE,
summarise() computes the count of observations, mean, standard deviation and standard error of Maths by RACE,
mutate() is used to derive standard error of Maths by RACE, and
save the output as a tibble data table called my_sum and displaying it as a tibble dataframe in html table.



Show the code
my_sum <- exam %>% 
      group_by(RACE) %>% 
      summarise(n=n(),
                mean=mean(MATHS),
                sd = sd(MATHS)) %>% 
      mutate(se=sd/sqrt(n-1))   #<<< standard error formula

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
section: Clarification between SE Bars vs Confidence Interval (CI)
text:
Clarification between SE Bars vs Confidence Interval (CI)






Note



SE Bars vs Confidence Interval (CI)
Standard Error (SE) bars and Confidence Intervals (CI) both visualise uncertainty around a mean, but they differ in interpretation:

SE bars show \(\bar{x} \pm SE\) — they represent the precision of the sample mean as an estimate of the population mean. They are narrower and do not directly tell you the probability of capturing the true mean.
Confidence Intervals show \(\bar{x} \pm z \times SE\) — they represent a range that is likely to contain the true population mean with a specified probability (e.g. 95% or 99%). The multiplier \(z\) depends on the confidence level:

90% CI: \(z = 1.645\)
95% CI: \(z = 1.96\)
99% CI: \(z = 2.58\)











SE Bars
Confidence Interval




Formula
\(\bar{x} \pm SE\)
\(\bar{x} \pm z \times SE\)


Width
Narrower
Wider — depends on confidence level


Interpretation
Precision of sample mean
Range likely containing true population mean


Common usage
Quick visual comparison between groups
Formal statistical inference


Example
\(\bar{x} \pm 1.13\)
\(\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \times 1.13\) for 95% CI



In short — SE bars are a building block of CI. A wider CI means more uncertainty about where the true population mean lies.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
3.1 using ggplot2

(a) standard error bars(b) confidence interval



The code chunk below is used to reveal the standard error of mean maths score by race. It shows one standard deviation away from the ‘mother of all means’ for all the means from all the samples.
stat = 'identity' means that the y values in the geom_point layer correspond to the actual values in the data frame, rather than a summary statistic like mean or median.
The error bars are computed by using the formula mean+/-se.



Show the code
ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(x=RACE,
                    ymin=mean-se,
                    ymax=mean+se),
                width = 0.2,
                colour = 'black',
                alpha = 0.9,
                linewidth=0.5) +
    geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat = 'identity', #<<< actual points refer to mean 
             color='#e0218a', 
             size = 1.5,
             alpha = 1) + 
  labs(title="Standard error of mean maths score by race",
       x= "Race",
       y= "Mean") +   
  theme_bw()  












Instead of plotting the standard error bar of point estimates, we can also plot the confidence intervals of mean maths score by race.
Lets plot a 95% confidence interval of mean maths score by race. The error bars should be sorted by the average maths scores. (Refer to take-home ex 1 on sorting by mean)
The confidence intervals are computed by using the formula mean+/-1.96*se.
The error bars is sorted by using the average maths scores.



Show the code
ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x=reorder(RACE,-mean),  # reorder(x,y) means to reorder x based on increasing or decreasing values of y. To sort by descending values of Y, use -Y.
                    ymin=mean-1.96*se,    #<<<< formula to calc 95% CI
                    ymax=mean+ 1.96*se),  #<<<<
                width = 0.2,
                colour = 'black',
                alpha = 0.9,
                size=0.5) +
    geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat = 'identity', #<<< actual points refer to mean 
             color='red', 
             size = 1.5,
             alpha = 1) + 
  labs(x = "Maths score",
       title = "95% confidence interval of mean maths score by race") +
  theme_bw()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
3.2 using plotly

(a) plotly(b) ggirafe & plotly


What is Crosstalk?

Crosstalk is an R package that allows different interactive widgets to talk to each other — so when you interact with one widget (e.g. click a row in a table), another widget (e.g. a plot) automatically updates to reflect your selection.

What is SharedData?

Normally, a plot and a table each have their own separate copy of the data — they don’t know about each other. SharedData solves this by creating a single shared data object that both widgets read from simultaneously.



Show the code
# Step 1: Prepare summary statistics
my_sum <- exam %>% 
  group_by(RACE) %>% 
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean = mean(MATHS),
    sd = sd(MATHS)
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    se = sd / sqrt(n - 1),
    lower_99 = round(mean - 2.58 * se, 2),
    upper_99 = round(mean + 2.58 * se, 2)
  ) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 2)))

# Step 2: Create SharedData object for crosstalk
d <- highlight_key(my_sum, ~RACE)

# Step 3: Build the interactive plot
p <- ggplot(d) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x = reorder(RACE, -mean),
        ymin = lower_99,
        ymax = upper_99),
    width = 0.2,
    colour = "black",
    linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = reorder(RACE, -mean),
        y = mean,
        text = paste0(
          "Race: ", RACE,
          "<br>N: ", n,
          "<br>Mean: ", mean,
          "<br>99% CI: [", lower_99, ", ", upper_99, "]")),
    stat = "identity",
    color = "#e0218a",
    size = 3) +
  labs(
    x = "Race",
    y = "Mean Score") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0))

# Step 4: Convert to plotly and add title via layout
gg <- ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "99% Confidence Interval of<br>Mean Maths Score by Race",
      font = list(size = 13),
      x = 0,
      xanchor = "left"
    ),
    margin = list(t = 60)   # add top margin for title
  )

# Step 5: Build the interactive table
tbl <- DT::datatable(
  d,
  rownames = FALSE,
  class = "compact",
  width = "100%",
  colnames = c("Race", "N", "Mean", "SD", "SE",
               "Lower 99% CI", "Upper 99% CI"),
  options = list(
    pageLength = 5,
    scrollX = FALSE,
    dom = "t",
    columnDefs = list(
      list(width = "60px", targets = c(1, 2, 3, 4)),
      list(width = "80px", targets = c(5, 6))
    ))) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("mean", "sd", "se",
                          "lower_99", "upper_99"),
              digits = 2)

# Step 6: Combine plot and table using crosstalk
crosstalk::bscols(
  widths = c(6, 6),    # use widths argument instead of list(width=...)
  highlight(gg,
            on = "plotly_click",
            off = "plotly_doubleclick"),
  tbl
)



















the ggiraph chart is interactive — just hover over the points and error bars to see the tooltip and highlighting effect. It just doesn’t have a table!



Show the code
# Create tooltip text
my_sum <- my_sum %>%
  mutate(tooltip = paste0(
    "Race: ", RACE,
    "\nN: ", n,
    "\nMean: ", mean,
    "\n99% CI: [", lower_99, ", ", upper_99, "]"))

# Build ggiraph plot
p <- ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar_interactive(
    aes(x = reorder(RACE, -mean),
        ymin = lower_99,
        ymax = upper_99,
        tooltip = tooltip,
        data_id = RACE),
    width = 0.2,
    colour = "black",
    linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(x = reorder(RACE, -mean),
        y = mean,
        tooltip = tooltip,
        data_id = RACE),
    color = "#e0218a",
    size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "99% Confidence Interval of Mean Maths Score by Race",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Mean Score") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0))

# Render with girafe
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill:#c0392b; stroke:black;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;"),
    opts_tooltip(
      css = "background-color:white;
             color:black;
             font-size:12px;
             padding:8px;
             border-radius:5px;
             border:1px solid grey;")))

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
4 Visualising Uncertainty - using ggdist package

ggdist is an R package that provides a flexible set of ggplot2 geoms and stats designed especially for visualising distributions and uncertainty.
It is designed for both frequentist and Bayesian uncertainty visualization, taking the view that uncertainty visualization can be unified through the perspective of distribution visualization:

for frequentist models, one visualises confidence distributions or bootstrap distributions (see vignette(“freq-uncertainty-vis”));
for Bayesian models, one visualises probability distributions (see the tidybayes package, which builds on top of ggdist).



4.1 Visualising Mean and Confidence Interval (CI) - using ggdist package’s stats_point_pointinterval()

In the code chunk below, stat_pointinterval() of ggdist is used to build a visual for displaying distribution of maths scores by race.
stat_pointinterval means points and multiple intervals. The default confidence interval us 95%. To change the level to 99%, add conf.level = 0.99 to stat_pointinterval function.
Take note that the default .width values are set to c(0.66, 0.95) confidence intervals.


(a) Mean Point + CI (66%,95%)(b) Mean Point + CI (95%)(c) Mean Point + CT (95%, 99%)




Show the code
exam %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = MATHS,
             color = RACE,      
             fill = RACE)) +    
  stat_pointinterval(
    .width = c(0.66, 0.95),
    alpha = 0.6) +             
  labs(
    title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
    subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot 66% and 95%",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Math") +  
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +  
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +   
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")













Show the code
exam %>%       
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, 
             y = MATHS,
             color = RACE,
             fill = RACE)) +       
  stat_pointinterval(         
    .width = 0.95,
    .point = median,
    .interval = qi,
    alpha = 0.6,
    point_size = 3) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(     
    title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",     
    subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot, 95% only",        
    x = "Race",        
    y = "Math") +     
  theme_bw() +
  theme(     
    axis.title.y = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.line = element_line(linewidth = 0.2),      
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none")













Show the code
exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, 
             y = MATHS)) +
  stat_pointinterval(
    .width = c(0.95, 0.99),
    .point = median,
    .interval = qi,
    aes(interval_color = stat(level),
        color = RACE),
    point_size = 3,
    show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2",
                     aesthetics = "color") +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("0.95" = "#73b2c4", 
               "0.99" = "#f27279"),
    aesthetics = "interval_color") +
  labs(
    title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
    subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot, 95% and 99%",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Math") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    axis.title.y = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.line = element_line(linewidth = 0.2),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0, face = "bold"))














4.2 Visualising Mean and Confidence Interval (CI) - using ggdist package’s stat_gradientinterval()

In the code chunk below, stat_gradientinterval() of ggdist is used to build a visual for displaying distribution of maths scores by race.



Show the code
exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE, 
             y = MATHS,
             fill = RACE)) +
  stat_gradientinterval(   
    alpha = 0.7,
    show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title = "Visualising confidence intervals of mean math score",
    subtitle = "Gradient + interval plot",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Math") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    axis.title.y = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
section: 5 Visualing Uncertainy - using Hypothetical Outcome Plots (HOPs)
text:
5 Visualing Uncertainy - using Hypothetical Outcome Plots (HOPs)
Hypothetical Outcome Plots (HOPs) visualise uncertainty by showing a set of draws from a distribution, where each draw is displayed as a new plot in either small multiples or animated form — rather than showing a single continuous probability distribution.
How the code works:

geom_point() creates a scatterplot of MATHS scores against RACE, with position_jitter() adding slight random noise to avoid overplotting
factor(RACE) converts RACE to a categorical variable so it is treated as discrete groups on the x-axis
geom_hpline() draws a horizontal line representing the median MATHS score for each race, calculated from 25 bootstrap samples drawn using sampler(25, group = RACE)
transition_states(.draw, 1, 3) animates through each bootstrap sample draw — each frame shows a different sample’s median line, illustrating how the median estimate varies across samples
The rapid animation gives an intuitive visual sense of the uncertainty around the median — stable lines indicate low uncertainty, while wildly varying lines indicate high uncertainty



Show the code
hop <- ggplot(data = exam, 
              aes(x = factor(RACE), 
                  y = MATHS)) +
  
  # Background jittered points — individual student scores
  geom_point(position = position_jitter(height = 0.3, width = 0.05), 
             size = 1.2, 
             color = "#a6a6a6", 
             alpha = 0.4) +
  
  # Animated median line per bootstrap sample
  geom_hpline(data = sampler(25, group = RACE), 
              height = 0.6, 
              color = "#f5347f",
              linewidth = 1.2) +
  
  # Labels
  labs(
    title = "Hypothetical Outcome Plot of Maths Scores by Race",
    subtitle = "Each animated line represents a bootstrap sample median",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Math") +
  
  # Theme
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0, face = "bold", size = 12),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(hjust = 1, angle = 0),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  
  # Animation
  transition_states(.draw, 
                    transition_length = 1, 
                    state_length = 3)

animate(hop, 
        fps = 8, 
        duration = 10,
        width = 600,
        height = 400,
        units = "px",
        res = 96)

title: Hands-on Exercise 5a: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
Ternary plots are graphical tools used to visualise the distribution and variation of compositional data made up of three components. Examples include the proportions of young, economically active, and elderly populations, or the composition of sand, silt, and clay in soil samples. The plot is represented as a triangle scaled from 0 to 1 along each side, where each side corresponds to one of the three components. A data point is positioned such that its perpendicular distance to each side reflects the value of the respective component.
In this hands-on exercise, we will learn how to create ternary plots programmatically in R to analyse and visualise the population structure of Singapore.
The exercise is organised into four main steps:

Install and load the tidyverse and ggtern packages.
Create three new measures using the mutate() function from the dplyr package.
Construct a static ternary plot using the ggtern() function from the ggtern package.
Create an interactive ternary plot using the plot_ly() function from the Plotly R package.

title: Hands-on Exercise 5a: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
Ternary plots are graphical tools used to visualise the distribution and variation of compositional data made up of three components. Examples include the proportions of young, economically active, and elderly populations, or the composition of sand, silt, and clay in soil samples. The plot is represented as a triangle scaled from 0 to 1 along each side, where each side corresponds to one of the three components. A data point is positioned such that its perpendicular distance to each side reflects the value of the respective component.
In this hands-on exercise, we will learn how to create ternary plots programmatically in R to analyse and visualise the population structure of Singapore.
The exercise is organised into four main steps:

Install and load the tidyverse and ggtern packages.
Create three new measures using the mutate() function from the dplyr package.
Construct a static ternary plot using the ggtern() function from the ggtern package.
Create an interactive ternary plot using the plot_ly() function from the Plotly R package.

title: Hands-on Exercise 5a: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
2 Getting Started

Installing & Launching R PackagesImporting DataPreparing Data


In this exercise, we will use two main R packages:

ggtern, an extension of ggplot2 designed specifically for creating ternary diagrams. We will use this package to build static ternary plots.
Plotly R, an R package for developing interactive web-based visualisations using Plotly’s JavaScript graphing library, plotly.js. We will use the ggplotly() function to convert ggplot2 graphics into interactive Plotly objects.



Show the code
pacman::p_load(plotly, tidyverse, ggtern, tricolore, gganimate)




The original data is obtained from Singapore Residents by Planning AreaSubzone, Age Group, Sex and Type of Dwelling, June 2000-2025 off the Singstat website.


Show the code
#Reading the data into R environment
pop_data <- read_csv("data/respopagesextod2025.csv") 




The code chunk below performs several tasks:

Uses as.character() to convert the Year variable into the character data type.
Uses spread() to transform key-value pairs into multiple columns, similar to a simpler version of pivot_wider().
Syntax: spread(data, key, value), where:

data refers to the data frame,
key specifies the column whose values will become new column names, and
value specifies the column containing the values to populate the new columns.

Uses the mutate() function from the dplyr package to create three new measures: young, active, and old.



Show the code
agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Sex == "Males") %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`,`50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`, 
                               `85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025) %>%
  filter(TOTAL > 0)

title: Hands-on Exercise 5a: Creating Ternary Plot with R
text:
3 Plotting Ternary Diagram with R

(a) Basic Plot(b) Subplots + tricolore(c) Interactive(d) gganimate(e) Animated


Here, we use ggtern() function of ggtern package to create a simple ternary plot. We also style it with color and title.


Show the code
ggtern(data = agpop_mutated,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD, colour = YOUNG)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Young Population") +
  labs(title = "Population Structure, 2025") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    legend.background = element_rect(colour = "grey93", fill = "grey93"),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "grey93", colour = "grey93")
  )











The following code filter the data for male and female.


Show the code
library(tricolore)
library(cowplot)

agpop_male <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Sex == "Males") %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`, `50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`,
                               `85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025, TOTAL > 0)

agpop_male$tc_color <- Tricolore(agpop_male, p1 = "YOUNG", p2 = "ACTIVE", p3 = "OLD")$color

agpop_female <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Sex == "Females") %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`, `50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`,
                               `85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025, TOTAL > 0)

agpop_female$tc_color <- Tricolore(agpop_female, p1 = "YOUNG", p2 = "ACTIVE", p3 = "OLD")$color


Normalise proportions and regenerate Tricolore colors


Show the code
agpop_male <- agpop_male %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG_P  = YOUNG / TOTAL,
    ACTIVE_P = ACTIVE / TOTAL,
    OLD_P    = OLD / TOTAL
  )

agpop_female <- agpop_female %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG_P  = YOUNG / TOTAL,
    ACTIVE_P = ACTIVE / TOTAL,
    OLD_P    = OLD / TOTAL
  )

agpop_male$tc_color   <- Tricolore(agpop_male,   p1 = "YOUNG_P", p2 = "ACTIVE_P", p3 = "OLD_P")$rgb
agpop_female$tc_color <- Tricolore(agpop_female, p1 = "YOUNG_P", p2 = "ACTIVE_P", p3 = "OLD_P")$rgb


Next, we create the ternary diagrams for male and female side-by-side using layout-ncol=2

Show the code
male_colors <- agpop_male$tc_color   #<< extract first

ggtern(data = agpop_male,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point(color = male_colors,    #<< plain vector
             size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Males, 2025", x = "Young", y = "Active", z = "Old") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
female_colors <- agpop_female$tc_color  #<< extract first

ggtern(data = agpop_female,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point(color = female_colors,     #<< plain vector
             size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Females, 2025", x = "Young", y = "Active", z = "Old") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))



























Show the code
paxis <- function(txt) {
  list(
    title = txt,
    tickformat = ".0%",
    tickfont = list(size = 10),
    titlefont = list(size = 12),
    gridcolor = "lightgrey",
    linecolor = "black",
    linewidth = 1
  )
}

ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = paxis("Young"),
  baxis = paxis("Economically Active"),
  caxis = paxis("Aged")
)

plot_ly() %>%
  add_trace(
    data = agpop_male,
    a = ~YOUNG_P, b = ~ACTIVE_P, c = ~OLD_P,
    color = I("steelblue"),
    type = "scatterternary",
    mode = "markers",
    marker = list(size = 6, opacity = 0.7),
    text = ~paste("PA:", PA, "<br>SZ:", SZ),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    subplot = "ternary"
  ) %>%
  add_trace(
    data = agpop_female,
    a = ~YOUNG_P, b = ~ACTIVE_P, c = ~OLD_P,
    color = I("tomato"),
    type = "scatterternary",
    mode = "markers",
    marker = list(size = 6, opacity = 0.7),
    text = ~paste("PA:", PA, "<br>SZ:", SZ),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    subplot = "ternary2"
  ) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Population Structure by Gender, 2025",
      font = list(size = 14, family = "Arial")
    ),
    
    ## ----- ADJUST THE GAP BETWEEN THE 2 TRIANGLES ---
ternary  = c(ternaryAxes, list(domain = list(x = c(0.05, 0.42), y = c(0, 0.9)))),
ternary2 = c(ternaryAxes, list(domain = list(x = c(0.62, 0.97), y = c(0, 0.9)))),
    
    ## ----------
annotations = list(list(text = "Males, 2025",   x = 0.15, y = 0.98, xref = "paper", yref = "paper", showarrow = FALSE, font = list(size = 13),
bgcolor = "white", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 1.5),
list(text = "Females, 2025", x = 0.82, y = 0.98, xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
showarrow = FALSE, font = list(size = 13),
bgcolor = "white", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 1.5)),
paper_bgcolor = "white",
showlegend = FALSE,
margin = list(t = 80, r = 100, l = 80, b = 60)  #<< right margin to prevent truncation
  )








Here, we shall use gganimate() to create an animated plot.
First, let’s filter the data. Since this dataset has only one year of data (2025), we shall animate using ‘Type of Dwelling (TOD)’ column.


Show the code
agpop_animated <- pop_data %>%
  spread(AG, Pop) %>%                                    #<< spread first
  mutate(across(`0_to_4`:`90_and_over`, ~replace_na(., 0))) %>%
  group_by(PA, SZ, TOD, Time) %>%
  summarise(
    across(`0_to_4`:`90_and_over`, sum),                 #<< then sum by group
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    YOUNG  = rowSums(select(., `0_to_4`, `5_to_9`, `10_to_14`, `15_to_19`, `20_to_24`), na.rm = TRUE),
    ACTIVE = rowSums(select(., `25_to_29`, `30_to_34`, `35_to_39`, `40_to_44`, `45_to_49`,`50_to_54`, `55_to_59`, `60_to_64`), na.rm = TRUE),
    OLD    = rowSums(select(., `65_to_69`, `70_to_74`, `75_to_79`, `80_to_84`,
`85_to_89`, `90_and_over`), na.rm = TRUE),
    TOTAL  = YOUNG + ACTIVE + OLD
  ) %>%
  filter(Time == 2025, TOTAL > 0)


Next we animate the plot


Show the code
library(magick)

tod_list <- unique(agpop_animated$TOD)

frames <- lapply(tod_list, function(tod) {
  df <- agpop_animated %>% filter(TOD == tod)
  
  p <- ggtern(data = df,
              aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD,
                  size = TOTAL, color = PA)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
    scale_size(range = c(2, 15)) +
    scale_L_continuous("Young\n(0-24)") +
    scale_T_continuous("Working Age\n(25-64)") +
    scale_R_continuous("Aged\n(65+)") +
    labs(
      title = "Singapore Population Age Structure by Dwelling Type, 2025",
      subtitle = paste("Dwelling Type:", tod)
    ) +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(
      tern.axis.arrow.show = TRUE,
      tern.axis.text    = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey20"),
      tern.axis.title.L = element_text(hjust = 0.2, vjust = 0.7, angle = -60),
      tern.axis.title.R = element_text(hjust = 0.8, vjust = 0.6, angle = 60),
      plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none"
    )
  
  tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".png")
  ggsave(tmp, p, width = 7, height = 6, dpi = 100)
  image_read(tmp)
})

animation <- image_join(frames)
image_animate(animation, fps = 1)











Lastly, here’s the chart using plotly animation.


Show the code
td3 <- plot_ly(agpop_animated,
               a = ~ACTIVE,
               b = ~YOUNG,
               c = ~OLD,
               frame = ~TOD,
               mode = "markers",
               size = ~TOTAL,
               color = ~PA,
               type = "scatterternary",
               text = ~paste("Young:", YOUNG,
                             "<br>Economically Active:", ACTIVE,
                             "<br>Aged:", OLD,
                             "<br>Subzone:", SZ,
                             "<br>Planning Area:", PA),
               hoverinfo = "text",
               marker = list(
                 symbol = "circle",
                 opacity = 0.8,
                 sizemode = "diameter",
                 sizeref = 2,
                 line = list(width = 2, color = "#FFFFFF")
               )) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Demographic Composition of Singapore by Dwelling Type, 2025",
      font = list(size = 14, family = "Arial"),
      x = 0.5,
      y = 0.98                                      #<< push title up
    ),
    ternary = list(
      aaxis = list(title = "Economically Active",
                   tickformat = ".0%",
                   gridcolor = "lightgrey",
                   linecolor = "black"),
      baxis = list(title = "Young",
                   tickformat = ".0%",
                   gridcolor = "lightgrey",
                   linecolor = "black"),
      caxis = list(title = "Aged",
                   tickformat = ".0%",
                   gridcolor = "lightgrey",
                   linecolor = "black"),
      domain = list(x = c(0, 1), y = c(0, 0.88))   #<< shrink ternary down
    ),
    paper_bgcolor = "white",
    plot_bgcolor  = "white",
    margin = list(t = 80)                           #<< top margin
  )
